3: By definition, environmental costs are those costs associated with environmental damages (Bickel and Friedrich, 2005) such as damages to humans, ecosystems and resources. They are also referred to as external costs or externalities since although considered as real costs, they do not show up on the bottom line of the company or country that is responsible for them. The price of meat, a product as any other products in the supermarket, typically includes only private costs, i.e. the costs paid by the producer for variable inputs e.g. raw materials, energy, water, labor, capital, etc., and totally excludes external costs (Field and Olewiler, 2005). For the meat price to be a complete reflection of the real costs to society, the costs of impacts across the meat chain from 'farm to fork' should be taken into account. This would guide livestock producers and processors, i.e. those involved in the entire meat production cycle, in selecting production/processing alternatives that are more environmentally friendly and thus more sustainable in the long run. In fact, when one unit (e.g. 1 kg) of meat leaves the farm for subsequent processes until ready for sale to consumers in the supermarket, it has accomplished a major part of its life cycle. This also holds for environmental load where research has shown
that, for livestock products, a major share of many impact cate- gories is from the primary production and the related inputs (Nielsen et al., 2003; Wognum et al., 2009).

Many studies on externalities of energy production systems have been conducted over the last ten years, based on monetar- ization of emissions of CO2, CO, NOx, SOx, volatile organic compounds and particulates that contribute to various environ- mental impacts (Friedrich et al., 2001; Kagel and Gawell, 2005; Krajnc et al., 2007; Nguyen and Gheewala, 2008; Owen, 2006; Wang et al., 2010). Two major problems that most environmental externalities assessments have been facing are, first of all, the high uncertainty in relation to monetarization of different environ- mental impacts and second, incompleteness as not all relevant environmental impacts are monetarized (see comments from e.g. Bos and Vleugel, 2005; Turner et al., 2004). As an example of this incompleteness matter, we can refer to the study of Krajnc et al. (2007) which considered five impact categories in estimating externalities: climate change, acidification, eutrophication, photo- chemical ozone creation and human toxicity. Recently, the Step- wise2006 method (Weidema, 2009) has been developed aiming at reducing the previously encountered uncertainty and incomplete- ness in monetarizing a wide range of environmental impacts (up to 15 different impact categories are included) thus enhancing the applicability and practicability of cost-benefit analyses. In the method, damage categories e.g. human health, ecosystem quality, and resource productivity considered by the Eco-indicator99

(Goedkoop and Spriensma, 2001) are redefined in terms of Quality Adjusted Life Years (QALY), Biodiversity Adjusted Hectare Years (BAHY), and monetary units (EUR), respectively. The monetary values of a QALY and a BAHY are derived thus allowing impact assessment results at midpoint to be translated into monetary units as endpoint. To test for accuracy, the uncertainty of the two monetary conversion factors is determined and presented in the related article (i.e. Weidema, 2009).

The objective of this study is two-fold: first, to quantify the environmental costs of pork production using a life cycle approach; second, to assess the potential to reduce the costs by combining improvement measures in three aspects: feed use, manure management, and manure utilization which were highlighted in a recent study (Nguyen et al., 2010a). We select conventional pork production in the EU as a case study for our analysis. The envi- ronmental costs are estimated via characterized results at midpoint categories (e.g. global warming, acidification, eutrophication, etc.) multiplied with monetary conversion factors available from the Stepwise2006 method.

7: <header>2. Materials and life cycle impact assessment (LCIA) method </header>
2.1. Pig production in the EU, functional unit and assessment parameters
8: <header>2.1.1. System description: common practice and potential improvement in environmental performance </header>

In Fig. 1 is presented the system boundary for typical intensive pig production. It includes the main sub-system, i.e. pig farming consisting primarily of animal housing and manure management/ manure utilization, and various associated sub-processes such as the production of feed, transportation of feed and the production of energy (heat and electricity) consumed at the farm. The key
assumptions used in the analysis in relation to feed use and manure management/manure utilization in the system are described below.

The pig feed in real life is complex in terms of ingredients of different sources but for the purpose of optimizing the feed ration from a biological and economical point of view, it can be general- ized that the pork production is global and draw on global feed resources. Thus producing an extra pig, as pointed out by Bouwman et al. (2006), will ultimately draw on resources of protein feed (e.g. oilseed meal) and energy feed (e.g. wheat, barley). The amounts of oilseed meal (mainly soy meal, according to Dalgaard et al., 2008) and grain cereals are modeled based on protein and energy content of the feed ingredients needed to satisfy the protein and energy requirements of the pigs. The requirement for these two feed components also implies the requirement for land which is routinely assessed within life cycle assessment in the impact category 'land use'. There has been interest in the relationship between improved feed efficiency and a reduction in the environ- mental impacts of livestock production (Stalder et al., 2004). Besides soy meal and grain, pig's diet also contains mineral phos- phorous and (a small amount of) copper as a feed supplement and a growth promoting agent, respectively. The inclusion of copper in pig's diet results in a certain concentration of copper in the manure since a very small portion of the metal is retained in the meat. When manure is applied to soil, copper released from manure to soil and water contributes a toxic agent to aquatic life (Weidema et al., 2008).

The manure excreted by the animals in the form of slurry i.e. a mixture of liquid and solid particles is first stored in the pit beneath the slats for a short interval and then pumped to the external storage tank where it is ready for next steps e.g. field application, anaerobic digestion. Regular (e.g. monthly) removal of manure from storage pits inside the building and proper storage of
Fig. 1. Overview of the product chain of pork at farm level (adapted from Nguyen et al., 2010a). Remark: Dotted lines indicate alternative process flows for improvement.
manure in outdoor slurry tanks with a natural crust cover are essential to environmentally friendly manure management in livestock production.

The manure after storage, considered as a resource, is most likely intended to be used as fertilizers elsewhere outside the farm. In any case due to environmental regulations and sound reasoning, this manure will substitute synthetic fertilizers to some extent depending on the actual availability for crops. The substitution rate for the three major nutritional elements in manure, nitrogen, phosphorous and potassium, is supposed to reach 75, 100 and 100%, respectively (Nguyen et al., 2010a). Besides having fertilizer value, manure has also been appreciated for its value as an energy source. This can be realized by converting manure to biogas for heat and electricity and the degassed manure is returned to the fields for crop production (see dotted lines in Fig. 1). We argue that all environmental impacts related to manure during housing, storage, and utilization should be allocated to the pork production (inde- pendently of whether it appears on the particular pig farm or not), while taking into account the environmental benefits of the avoi- ded products e.g. fertilizer nutrients, fossil-based heat and electricity.

Fig. 1. Overview of the product chain of pork at farm level (adapted from Nguyen et al., 2010a). Remark: Dotted lines indicate alternative process flows for improvement.
manure in outdoor slurry tanks with a natural crust cover are essential to environmentally friendly manure management in livestock production.

The manure after storage, considered as a resource, is most likely intended to be used as fertilizers elsewhere outside the farm. In any case due to environmental regulations and sound reasoning, this manure will substitute synthetic fertilizers to some extent depending on the actual availability for crops. The substitution rate for the three major nutritional elements in manure, nitrogen, phosphorous and potassium, is supposed to reach 75, 100 and 100%, respectively (Nguyen et al., 2010a). Besides having fertilizer value, manure has also been appreciated for its value as an energy source. This can be realized by converting manure to biogas for heat and electricity and the degassed manure is returned to the fields for crop production (see dotted lines in Fig. 1). We argue that all environmental impacts related to manure during housing, storage, and utilization should be allocated to the pork production (inde- pendently of whether it appears on the particular pig farm or not), while taking into account the environmental benefits of the avoi- ded products e.g. fertilizer nutrients, fossil-based heat and electricity.
12: <header>2.1.2. Private costs of pig production in the EU </header>

The costs of primary pig production consist of three categories: feed costs, labor costs, and interest and depreciation, of which, 'feed costs' is regarded as the most important cost category. From the study by Rasmussen (2004) on pig production costs in 2000 in selected countries: Canada, the US, Ireland, France, Denmark, the Netherlands, Germany, Sweden and the UK, it shows that the costs are different from country to country, depending on many factors e.g. feed efficiency, feed prices, weight of the animal at slaughter, etc. Considering European countries, the costs however do not show a big variation, ranging from 1.2 EUR in Ireland to 1.5 EUR in the UK per kg carcass. For the purpose of comparing the size of the environmental costs with that of the private costs, it appears justified to use the average value of the production costs of pig meat in Europe, 1.4 EUR per kg carcass.
13: The functional unit used in the study is one kg pig meat carcass weight delivered from farms.
14: <header>2.1.4. Environmental externalities assessment method, assessment parameters and main data sources </header>

In this study, we use the Stepwise2006 method (Weidema, 2009) to estimate the environmental costs. The three steps involved in the method are summarized as follows. The first two steps namely life cycle inventory and characterization are common to life cycle impact analyses. In the life cycle inventory step, an inventory of resource use and emissions associated with the entire product life cycle is performed. In the characterization step, the inventory results are classified and grouped into various impact categories - often named mid-points - e.g. global warming, acid- ification, eutrophication, human toxicity, ecotoxicity, energy use, land use, etc. In literature regarding foods often the life cycle assessment ends with these impact categories (e.g. Cederberg and Mattson, 2000; Lundie and Peters, 2005; Nguyen et al., 2010b; Strid-Eriksson et al., 2005). The third step, weighting of the rela- tive importance of environmental impacts in monetary terms, is a key step in cost-benefit analysis in which different midpoint impact scores are converted to endpoint impact scores, using monetary conversion factors as shown in Table 1 (adapted from Weidema, 2009). This enables an aggregation of all midpoint impact scores into a total single score. The theoretical basis of the method is briefly described below.

The impact assessment method "Stepwise2006" provides a tool to fully monetarize all impacts and to express them in a single score while offering the option of presenting results at midpoint cate- gories specialized in environmental themes. For calculating char- acterized results at midpoint, the method combines the characterization models from the two recent impact assessment methods, the IMPACT2002+ V. 2.1 (Jolliet et al., 2003) and the EDIP2003 methods (Hauschild and Potting, 2005; Potting and Hauschild, 2005). In the method, each midpoint impact category is classified into three damage categories, human health, ecosystem quality and resources with the measurement unit Quality Adjusted Life Year (QALY), Biodiversity Adjusted Hectare Years (BAHY), and EUR, respectively, using different characterization factors (not shown here but available in Weidema et al., 2008). The monetar- ization of impacts on human well-being QALY is done using a consistent weighting method based on the budget constraint approach. As explained in the method description (Weidema, 2009), the monetary value of a QALY has an upper limit defined

PM: particulate matter, CFC: chlorofluorocarbon, TEG: triethylene glycol, UES: unprotected ecosystem.

a This "zero" factor is derived based on the assumption that the future energy system will be based on renewable energy sources. Current dissipation of non-renewable energy carriers thus is not expected to have influence on the future energy requirement for provision of energy (Weidema et al., 2008).

by the budget constraint and this is justified by the fact that the average annual income is the maximum that an average person can pay for an additional life year. The budget constraint is determined as the potential average annual income at full well-being, which is equal to the potential annual economic production per capita, amounting to 74,000 EUR with an uncertainty range of 62,000-84,000 EUR. Also, according to the same reference (Weidema, 2009), the value of ecosystems BAHY can be expressed conveniently in terms of QALYs or in monetary terms, being a share of human well-being that can be sacrificed for the sake of ecosystem protection. While stressing that this trade-off should preferably be done by choice modeling and that further studies are needed allowing for a better monetary evaluation of ecosystem impacts, the developers of the method suggest to use a temporary proxy value of 1400 EUR/BAHY (i.e. 52 BAHY/QALY) as the best available estimate at the time. This weighting factor is considered not a single value but likely to fall anywhere within a relatively large uncertainty range of 350-3500 EUR/BAHY. The upper bound of the range corresponds to the adjusted "exchange rate" between biodiversity and human well-being, 21 BAHY/QALY, whereas the lower bound is the reference value obtained from ExternE, a bottom-up impact pathway approach to assessing the external costs of emissions from energy production (Bickel and Friedrich, 2005).

From Table 1, we can see that the monetarization to assign monetary values to 7 impact categories, 'Ecotoxicity, aquatic', 'Ecotoxicity, terrestrial', 'Nature occupation', 'Global warming', 'Acidification', 'Eutrophication, aquatic', 'Eutrophication, terres- trial', is associated with a relatively large uncertainty. This is explained in light of the above-described matter of uncertainty and the observation that up to 98-100% of the monetary values in these cases is made up of ecosystem impact.

All calculations for the analysis are performed in SimaPro 7.1, using data mainly from Nielsen et al. (2003), Dalgaard et al. (2008), ETH-ESU (1996), and Ecoinvent Centre (2007) for key system inputs e.g. livestock feed, transportation, heat and electricity and outputs e.g. manure exported for crop fertilization and energy production.

21: <header>2.2. EU pig farming: baseline and abatement scenarios </header>

As per our objective, in addition to the baseline scenario, we include an abatement scenario which considers the potential of improvement in environmental cost reduction. In a recent study, Nguyen et al. (2010a) assessed energy and GHG performance of pig meat production in Europe and indicated that there is room for improvement. The authors highlighted feed use efficiency, manure management practice and manure utilization as important factors to be considered in developing improvement scenarios. The best- case scenario i.e. the scenario that maximizes the energy and GHG saving potentials according to their analysis is a combination of three improvement measures. First is reducing feed use by increasing feed efficiency; besides reducing the environmental impacts related to feed production and transport, a reduced feed use in terms of dry matter and nutrient elements also works in reducing emissions related to manure handling. Second is reducing emissions of methane, a potent GHG, from housing by shortening manure in-house storage duration; according to IPCC (2006), the level of methane emissions from housing in the same climatic condition is very much dependent on this time scale. The third measure is utilizing manure for energy production in addition to crop fertilization; the practice is beneficial in avoiding fossil-based heat and electricity production (Nielsen et al., 2003), reducing methane emissions during subsequent storage and nitrous oxide emissions during manure application (Sommer et al., 2004), and

improving nitrogen availability to crops (Birkmose, 2007). In our analysis, inventory results for the baseline are obtained directly from Nguyen et al. (2010a) whereas those for the abatement scenario are adapted from the best-case scenario in the same reference. They are presented in Table 2 in the two columns under the headings "Baseline scenario" and "Abatement scenario".
22: <header>2.2. EU pig farming: baseline and abatement scenarios </header>

As per our objective, in addition to the baseline scenario, we include an abatement scenario which considers the potential of improvement in environmental cost reduction. In a recent study, Nguyen et al. (2010a) assessed energy and GHG performance of pig meat production in Europe and indicated that there is room for improvement. The authors highlighted feed use efficiency, manure management practice and manure utilization as important factors to be considered in developing improvement scenarios. The best- case scenario i.e. the scenario that maximizes the energy and GHG saving potentials according to their analysis is a combination of three improvement measures. First is reducing feed use by increasing feed efficiency; besides reducing the environmental impacts related to feed production and transport, a reduced feed use in terms of dry matter and nutrient elements also works in reducing emissions related to manure handling. Second is reducing emissions of methane, a potent GHG, from housing by shortening manure in-house storage duration; according to IPCC (2006), the level of methane emissions from housing in the same climatic condition is very much dependent on this time scale. The third measure is utilizing manure for energy production in addition to crop fertilization; the practice is beneficial in avoiding fossil-based heat and electricity production (Nielsen et al., 2003), reducing methane emissions during subsequent storage and nitrous oxide emissions during manure application (Sommer et al., 2004), and

improving nitrogen availability to crops (Birkmose, 2007). In our analysis, inventory results for the baseline are obtained directly from Nguyen et al. (2010a) whereas those for the abatement scenario are adapted from the best-case scenario in the same reference. They are presented in Table 2 in the two columns under the headings "Baseline scenario" and "Abatement scenario".
23: <header>3.1. Environmental costs of pig meat in the baseline scenario </header>

Starting with the baseline scenario, Table 3 shows the midpoint and endpoint results for 16 impact categories and the external costs or environmental costs which are a sum of all impact scores. The endpoint results in relation to the three safeguard subjects, ecosystems, human well-being, and resource productivity, are also evaluated and aggregated into a single score (for each impact category and for all categories combined). In addition, Fig. 2 gives a pictorial observation of main contributing sub-processes among the listed sub-processes (e.g. feed production and transport, direct energy use, emissions from housing and manure storage, manure utilization for crop fertilization) and main contributing midpoint impact categories. Detailed discussions on the findings concerning the environmental costs of conventional pig meat as well as the main contributing sub-processes and main contributing impact categories are given below.

As a whole, the environmental costs of conventional pig meat are estimated at 1.9 EUR per kg, which are of even larger size than the private costs of 1.4 EUR. Interestingly, with a brief look, we observe that more than half of the costs are responsible by 'Nature occupation' (commonly referred to as 'Land use') associated with all process activities. This stresses the importance of land use impacts associated with agricultural production which have not been properly assessed in literature due mainly to methodological problems. A breakdown of the external costs into three damage categories shows that impacts on ecosystems obtain high impor- tance with 80% contribution to the costs whereas impacts on human well-being and impacts on resource productivity carry relatively low importance with 15% and 5% contribution, respectively.

As for the detailed information about on which part (or component) of the pig meat life cycle, the highest load of different environmental impacts occurs or the largest potential for improvements lies, further analysis is needed. Fig. 2 summarizes the results of such analysis. Clearly from the figure, feed production is an important contributor to 'Nature occupation' (100%), 'Eutro- phication, aquatic' (82.5%), 'Non-renewable energy' (69%), and 'Global warming' (55%). Emissions from housing and manure storage (primarily CH4, N20 and NH3) contribute a relatively large portion to 'Photochemical ozone, vegetation' (54.6%), 'Eutrophica- tion, terrestrial' (46.2%), and 'Acidification' (44%). In contrast, manure utilization for crop fertilization has a negative contribution to (i.e. a deduction from) the environmental costs of the pig meat product in three impact categories: Non-renewable energy (10%), Global warming (5.3%) and Photochemical ozone, vegetation (5.8%) but a positive contribution (i.e. an addition) in others, notably Ecotoxicity, aquatic (92%) and Human toxicity, non-carcinogens (44%). Feed transport is not a major contributor to all impact categories except for 'Ecotoxicity, terrestrial' where its contribution reaches 81%. Direct energy use with a 14% contribution to 'Non- renewable energy' and a less-than-5% contribution to other impact categories can also be considered a minor contributor to the overall environmental burden of the product.

production and the remaining is distributed among the other sub- processes, 'emissions from housing and manure storage', 'manure utilization for crop fertilization', 'feed transport' and 'direct energy use' by 16.3%, 3.2%, 3.7% and 1.2% respectively. The finding confirms that feed use and manure management are two key priorities for improvement. Making a breakdown of the costs related to each sub-process into three damage categories, we further find that impacts on ecosystems obtain relatively high importance for 'emissions from housing and manure storage', 'direct energy use' and especially 'feed production' with 57, 84 and 90% contribution respectively whereas impacts on human well-being tend to domi- nate the costs related to 'manure utilization for crop fertilization' with 76% contribution. Interestingly, the fact that feed production is the largest contributor to the environmental costs of pig meat production also holds for the private costs where the feed costs normally constitute more than 50% of the overall costs (Rasmussen, 2004).
In weighing impact categories against each other, 'Nature occupation' represents the main contributor (as mentioned earlier) with a share of 54.7% of the aggregated costs, followed by 'Global
warming', 'Respiratory inorganics', "Eutrophication, terrestrial' and others with a share of 21.2, 18.3, 3.1 and 2.7%, respectively.
3.2. Uncertainty of environmental cost estimates using the Stepwise2006 method

Table 3 shows that the monetarization of environmental exter- nalities of pig meat results in a large uncertainty range of 0.7-4.2 EUR, around the core estimate of 1.9 EUR/kg meat. This large uncertainty comes primarily from the cost fraction (80%) paid for damage on ecosystems where a translation into monetary terms is associated with an uncertainty range of 350-3500 EUR around the core value of 1400 EUR/BAHY (see Section 2.1.4). In the same manner, it appears that the environmental cost estimate in relation to 'feed production' and to 'direct energy use' is expected to have a large uncertainty while that related to 'manure utilization for crop fertilization' is relatively stable, considering the contribution of impacts on ecosystems and impacts on human to the costs related to each sub-process. 3.3. Environmental costs of pig production based on factors in the Stepwise2006 method versus factors used in previous studies

For a verification of the monetarization scheme used in the Stepwise2006 method versus those used in previous studies, Table 4 is constructed. In this table, we analyze life cycle emissions of 7 emission species in weight units per kg meat and convert them to environmental costs in monetary units using factors in Step- wise2006 versus those used in previous studies. The emissions we select for the comparison are the ones whose reference external cost values are available (Weidema, 2009).

<list>From Table 4, we can see that the sum of environmental costs related to 7 selected emissions based on Stepwise monetary values is 0.53 EUR/kg meat. The costs are within the reference range, 0.04-0.93 EUR/kg meat, despite the fact that different valuation approaches have been used to derive monetary conversion factors. Such a good agreement shows the reasonable applicability of the novel approach whereas an inclusion of environmental costs in relation to impacts of other emissions (e.g. copper, ammonia, nitrate, etc) and especially impact of land use proves its completeness over those used in previous studies.

production and the remaining is distributed among the other sub- processes, 'emissions from housing and manure storage', 'manure utilization for crop fertilization', 'feed transport' and 'direct energy use' by 16.3%, 3.2%, 3.7% and 1.2% respectively. The finding confirms that feed use and manure management are two key priorities for improvement. Making a breakdown of the costs related to each sub-process into three damage categories, we further find that impacts on ecosystems obtain relatively high importance for 'emissions from housing and manure storage', 'direct energy use' and especially 'feed production' with 57, 84 and 90% contribution respectively whereas impacts on human well-being tend to domi- nate the costs related to 'manure utilization for crop fertilization' with 76% contribution. Interestingly, the fact that feed production is the largest contributor to the environmental costs of pig meat production also holds for the private costs where the feed costs normally constitute more than 50% of the overall costs (Rasmussen, 2004).
In weighing impact categories against each other, 'Nature occupation' represents the main contributor (as mentioned earlier) with a share of 54.7% of the aggregated costs, followed by 'Global
warming', 'Respiratory inorganics', "Eutrophication, terrestrial' and others with a share of 21.2, 18.3, 3.1 and 2.7%, respectively.
3.2. Uncertainty of environmental cost estimates using the Stepwise2006 method

Table 3 shows that the monetarization of environmental exter- nalities of pig meat results in a large uncertainty range of 0.7-4.2 EUR, around the core estimate of 1.9 EUR/kg meat. This large uncertainty comes primarily from the cost fraction (80%) paid for damage on ecosystems where a translation into monetary terms is associated with an uncertainty range of 350-3500 EUR around the core value of 1400 EUR/BAHY (see Section 2.1.4). In the same manner, it appears that the environmental cost estimate in relation to 'feed production' and to 'direct energy use' is expected to have a large uncertainty while that related to 'manure utilization for crop fertilization' is relatively stable, considering the contribution of impacts on ecosystems and impacts on human to the costs related to each sub-process. 3.3. Environmental costs of pig production based on factors in the Stepwise2006 method versus factors used in previous studies

For a verification of the monetarization scheme used in the Stepwise2006 method versus those used in previous studies, Table 4 is constructed. In this table, we analyze life cycle emissions of 7 emission species in weight units per kg meat and convert them to environmental costs in monetary units using factors in Step- wise2006 versus those used in previous studies. The emissions we select for the comparison are the ones whose reference external cost values are available (Weidema, 2009).

<list>From Table 4, we can see that the sum of environmental costs related to 7 selected emissions based on Stepwise monetary values is 0.53 EUR/kg meat. The costs are within the reference range, 0.04-0.93 EUR/kg meat, despite the fact that different valuation approaches have been used to derive monetary conversion factors. Such a good agreement shows the reasonable applicability of the novel approach whereas an inclusion of environmental costs in relation to impacts of other emissions (e.g. copper, ammonia, nitrate, etc) and especially impact of land use proves its completeness over those used in previous studies.

25: <header>3.1. Environmental costs of pig meat in the baseline scenario </header>

Starting with the baseline scenario, Table 3 shows the midpoint and endpoint results for 16 impact categories and the external costs or environmental costs which are a sum of all impact scores. The endpoint results in relation to the three safeguard subjects, ecosystems, human well-being, and resource productivity, are also evaluated and aggregated into a single score (for each impact category and for all categories combined). In addition, Fig. 2 gives a pictorial observation of main contributing sub-processes among the listed sub-processes (e.g. feed production and transport, direct energy use, emissions from housing and manure storage, manure utilization for crop fertilization) and main contributing midpoint impact categories. Detailed discussions on the findings concerning the environmental costs of conventional pig meat as well as the main contributing sub-processes and main contributing impact categories are given below.

As a whole, the environmental costs of conventional pig meat are estimated at 1.9 EUR per kg, which are of even larger size than the private costs of 1.4 EUR. Interestingly, with a brief look, we observe that more than half of the costs are responsible by 'Nature occupation' (commonly referred to as 'Land use') associated with all process activities. This stresses the importance of land use impacts associated with agricultural production which have not been properly assessed in literature due mainly to methodological problems. A breakdown of the external costs into three damage categories shows that impacts on ecosystems obtain high impor- tance with 80% contribution to the costs whereas impacts on human well-being and impacts on resource productivity carry relatively low importance with 15% and 5% contribution, respectively.

As for the detailed information about on which part (or component) of the pig meat life cycle, the highest load of different environmental impacts occurs or the largest potential for improvements lies, further analysis is needed. Fig. 2 summarizes the results of such analysis. Clearly from the figure, feed production is an important contributor to 'Nature occupation' (100%), 'Eutro- phication, aquatic' (82.5%), 'Non-renewable energy' (69%), and 'Global warming' (55%). Emissions from housing and manure storage (primarily CH4, N20 and NH3) contribute a relatively large portion to 'Photochemical ozone, vegetation' (54.6%), 'Eutrophica- tion, terrestrial' (46.2%), and 'Acidification' (44%). In contrast, manure utilization for crop fertilization has a negative contribution to (i.e. a deduction from) the environmental costs of the pig meat product in three impact categories: Non-renewable energy (10%), Global warming (5.3%) and Photochemical ozone, vegetation (5.8%) but a positive contribution (i.e. an addition) in others, notably Ecotoxicity, aquatic (92%) and Human toxicity, non-carcinogens (44%). Feed transport is not a major contributor to all impact categories except for 'Ecotoxicity, terrestrial' where its contribution reaches 81%. Direct energy use with a 14% contribution to 'Non- renewable energy' and a less-than-5% contribution to other impact categories can also be considered a minor contributor to the overall environmental burden of the product.

This stresses the importance of land use impacts associated with agricultural production which have not been properly assessed in literature due mainly to methodological problems. A breakdown of the external costs into three damage categories shows that impacts on ecosystems obtain high impor- tance with 80% contribution to the costs whereas impacts on human well-being and impacts on resource productivity carry relatively low importance with 15% and 5% contribution, respectively.

As for the detailed information about on which part (or component) of the pig meat life cycle, the highest load of different environmental impacts occurs or the largest potential for improvements lies, further analysis is needed. Fig. 2 summarizes the results of such analysis. Clearly from the figure, feed production is an important contributor to 'Nature occupation' (100%), 'Eutro- phication, aquatic' (82.5%), 'Non-renewable energy' (69%), and 'Global warming' (55%). Emissions from housing and manure storage (primarily CH4, N20 and NH3) contribute a relatively large portion to 'Photochemical ozone, vegetation' (54.6%), 'Eutrophica- tion, terrestrial' (46.2%), and 'Acidification' (44%). In contrast, manure utilization for crop fertilization has a negative contribution to (i.e. a deduction from) the environmental costs of the pig meat product in three impact categories: Non-renewable energy (10%), Global warming (5.3%) and Photochemical ozone, vegetation (5.8%) but a positive contribution (i.e. an addition) in others, notably Ecotoxicity, aquatic (92%) and Human toxicity, non-carcinogens (44%). Feed transport is not a major contributor to all impact categories except for 'Ecotoxicity, terrestrial' where its contribution reaches 81%. Direct energy use with a 14% contribution to 'Non- renewable energy' and a less-than-5% contribution to other impact categories can also be considered a minor contributor to the overall environmental burden of the product.

production and the remaining is distributed among the other sub- processes, 'emissions from housing and manure storage', 'manure utilization for crop fertilization', 'feed transport' and 'direct energy use' by 16.3%, 3.2%, 3.7% and 1.2% respectively. The finding confirms that feed use and manure management are two key priorities for improvement. Making a breakdown of the costs related to each sub-process into three damage categories, we further find that impacts on ecosystems obtain relatively high importance for 'emissions from housing and manure storage', 'direct energy use' and especially 'feed production' with 57, 84 and 90% contribution respectively whereas impacts on human well-being tend to domi- nate the costs related to 'manure utilization for crop fertilization' with 76% contribution. Interestingly, the fact that feed production is the largest contributor to the environmental costs of pig meat production also holds for the private costs where the feed costs normally constitute more than 50% of the overall costs (Rasmussen, 2004).
In weighing impact categories against each other, 'Nature occupation' represents the main contributor (as mentioned earlier) with a share of 54.7% of the aggregated costs, followed by 'Global
warming', 'Respiratory inorganics', "Eutrophication, terrestrial' and others with a share of 21.2, 18.3, 3.1 and 2.7%, respectively.
3.2. Uncertainty of environmental cost estimates using the Stepwise2006 method

Table 3 shows that the monetarization of environmental exter- nalities of pig meat results in a large uncertainty range of 0.7-4.2 EUR, around the core estimate of 1.9 EUR/kg meat. This large uncertainty comes primarily from the cost fraction (80%) paid for damage on ecosystems where a translation into monetary terms is associated with an uncertainty range of 350-3500 EUR around the core value of 1400 EUR/BAHY (see Section 2.1.4). In the same manner, it appears that the environmental cost estimate in relation to 'feed production' and to 'direct energy use' is expected to have a large uncertainty while that related to 'manure utilization for crop fertilization' is relatively stable, considering the contribution of impacts on ecosystems and impacts on human to the costs related to each sub-process. 3.3. Environmental costs of pig production based on factors in the Stepwise2006 method versus factors used in previous studies

For a verification of the monetarization scheme used in the Stepwise2006 method versus those used in previous studies, Table 4 is constructed. In this table, we analyze life cycle emissions of 7 emission species in weight units per kg meat and convert them to environmental costs in monetary units using factors in Step- wise2006 versus those used in previous studies. The emissions we select for the comparison are the ones whose reference external cost values are available (Weidema, 2009).

<list>From Table 4, we can see that the sum of environmental costs related to 7 selected emissions based on Stepwise monetary values is 0.53 EUR/kg meat. The costs are within the reference range, 0.04-0.93 EUR/kg meat, despite the fact that different valuation approaches have been used to derive monetary conversion factors. Such a good agreement shows the reasonable applicability of the novel approach whereas an inclusion of environmental costs in relation to impacts of other emissions (e.g. copper, ammonia, nitrate, etc) and especially impact of land use proves its completeness over those used in previous studies.

Starting with the baseline scenario, Table 3 shows the midpoint and endpoint results for 16 impact categories and the external costs or environmental costs which are a sum of all impact scores. The endpoint results in relation to the three safeguard subjects, ecosystems, human well-being, and resource productivity, are also evaluated and aggregated into a single score (for each impact category and for all categories combined). In addition, Fig. 2 gives a pictorial observation of main contributing sub-processes among the listed sub-processes (e.g. feed production and transport, direct energy use, emissions from housing and manure storage, manure utilization for crop fertilization) and main contributing midpoint impact categories. Detailed discussions on the findings concerning the environmental costs of conventional pig meat as well as the main contributing sub-processes and main contributing impact categories are given below.

As a whole, the environmental costs of conventional pig meat are estimated at 1.9 EUR per kg, which are of even larger size than the private costs of 1.4 EUR. Interestingly, with a brief look, we observe that more than half of the costs are responsible by 'Nature occupation' (commonly referred to as 'Land use') associated with all process activities. This stresses the importance of land use impacts associated with agricultural production which have not been properly assessed in literature due mainly to methodological problems. A breakdown of the external costs into three damage categories shows that impacts on ecosystems obtain high impor- tance with 80% contribution to the costs whereas impacts on human well-being and impacts on resource productivity carry relatively low importance with 15% and 5% contribution, respectively.

As for the detailed information about on which part (or component) of the pig meat life cycle, the highest load of different environmental impacts occurs or the largest potential for improvements lies, further analysis is needed. Fig. 2 summarizes the results of such analysis. Clearly from the figure, feed production is an important contributor to 'Nature occupation' (100%), 'Eutro- phication, aquatic' (82.5%), 'Non-renewable energy' (69%), and 'Global warming' (55%). Emissions from housing and manure storage (primarily CH4, N20 and NH3) contribute a relatively large portion to 'Photochemical ozone, vegetation' (54.6%), 'Eutrophica- tion, terrestrial' (46.2%), and 'Acidification' (44%). In contrast, manure utilization for crop fertilization has a negative contribution to (i.e. a deduction from) the environmental costs of the pig meat product in three impact categories: Non-renewable energy (10%), Global warming (5.3%) and Photochemical ozone, vegetation (5.8%) but a positive contribution (i.e. an addition) in others, notably Ecotoxicity, aquatic (92%) and Human toxicity, non-carcinogens (44%). Feed transport is not a major contributor to all impact categories except for 'Ecotoxicity, terrestrial' where its contribution reaches 81%. Direct energy use with a 14% contribution to 'Non- renewable energy' and a less-than-5% contribution to other impact categories can also be considered a minor contributor to the overall environmental burden of the product.

3.3. Environmental costs of pig production based on factors in the Stepwise2006 method versus factors used in previous studies

For a verification of the monetarization scheme used in the Stepwise2006 method versus those used in previous studies, Table 4 is constructed. In this table, we analyze life cycle emissions of 7 emission species in weight units per kg meat and convert them to environmental costs in monetary units using factors in Step- wise2006 versus those used in previous studies. The emissions we select for the comparison are the ones whose reference external cost values are available (Weidema, 2009).

<list>From Table 4, we can see that the sum of environmental costs related to 7 selected emissions based on Stepwise monetary values is 0.53 EUR/kg meat. The costs are within the reference range, 0.04-0.93 EUR/kg meat, despite the fact that different valuation approaches have been used to derive monetary conversion factors. Such a good agreement shows the reasonable applicability of the novel approach whereas an inclusion of environmental costs in relation to impacts of other emissions (e.g. copper, ammonia, nitrate, etc) and especially impact of land use proves its completeness over those used in previous studies.

28: <header>3.1. Environmental costs of pig meat in the baseline scenario </header>

Starting with the baseline scenario, Table 3 shows the midpoint and endpoint results for 16 impact categories and the external costs or environmental costs which are a sum of all impact scores. The endpoint results in relation to the three safeguard subjects, ecosystems, human well-being, and resource productivity, are also evaluated and aggregated into a single score (for each impact category and for all categories combined). In addition, Fig. 2 gives a pictorial observation of main contributing sub-processes among the listed sub-processes (e.g. feed production and transport, direct energy use, emissions from housing and manure storage, manure utilization for crop fertilization) and main contributing midpoint impact categories. Detailed discussions on the findings concerning the environmental costs of conventional pig meat as well as the main contributing sub-processes and main contributing impact categories are given below.

As a whole, the environmental costs of conventional pig meat are estimated at 1.9 EUR per kg, which are of even larger size than the private costs of 1.4 EUR. Interestingly, with a brief look, we observe that more than half of the costs are responsible by 'Nature occupation' (commonly referred to as 'Land use') associated with all process activities. This stresses the importance of land use impacts associated with agricultural production which have not been properly assessed in literature due mainly to methodological problems. A breakdown of the external costs into three damage categories shows that impacts on ecosystems obtain high impor- tance with 80% contribution to the costs whereas impacts on human well-being and impacts on resource productivity carry relatively low importance with 15% and 5% contribution, respectively.

As for the detailed information about on which part (or component) of the pig meat life cycle, the highest load of different environmental impacts occurs or the largest potential for improvements lies, further analysis is needed. Fig. 2 summarizes the results of such analysis. Clearly from the figure, feed production is an important contributor to 'Nature occupation' (100%), 'Eutro- phication, aquatic' (82.5%), 'Non-renewable energy' (69%), and 'Global warming' (55%). Emissions from housing and manure storage (primarily CH4, N20 and NH3) contribute a relatively large portion to 'Photochemical ozone, vegetation' (54.6%), 'Eutrophica- tion, terrestrial' (46.2%), and 'Acidification' (44%). In contrast, manure utilization for crop fertilization has a negative contribution to (i.e. a deduction from) the environmental costs of the pig meat product in three impact categories: Non-renewable energy (10%), Global warming (5.3%) and Photochemical ozone, vegetation (5.8%) but a positive contribution (i.e. an addition) in others, notably Ecotoxicity, aquatic (92%) and Human toxicity, non-carcinogens (44%). Feed transport is not a major contributor to all impact categories except for 'Ecotoxicity, terrestrial' where its contribution reaches 81%. Direct energy use with a 14% contribution to 'Non- renewable energy' and a less-than-5% contribution to other impact categories can also be considered a minor contributor to the overall environmental burden of the product.

production and the remaining is distributed among the other sub- processes, 'emissions from housing and manure storage', 'manure utilization for crop fertilization', 'feed transport' and 'direct energy use' by 16.3%, 3.2%, 3.7% and 1.2% respectively. The finding confirms that feed use and manure management are two key priorities for improvement. Making a breakdown of the costs related to each sub-process into three damage categories, we further find that impacts on ecosystems obtain relatively high importance for 'emissions from housing and manure storage', 'direct energy use' and especially 'feed production' with 57, 84 and 90% contribution respectively whereas impacts on human well-being tend to domi- nate the costs related to 'manure utilization for crop fertilization' with 76% contribution. Interestingly, the fact that feed production is the largest contributor to the environmental costs of pig meat production also holds for the private costs where the feed costs normally constitute more than 50% of the overall costs (Rasmussen, 2004).
In weighing impact categories against each other, 'Nature occupation' represents the main contributor (as mentioned earlier) with a share of 54.7% of the aggregated costs, followed by 'Global
warming', 'Respiratory inorganics', "Eutrophication, terrestrial' and others with a share of 21.2, 18.3, 3.1 and 2.7%, respectively.
3.2. Uncertainty of environmental cost estimates using the Stepwise2006 method

Table 3 shows that the monetarization of environmental exter- nalities of pig meat results in a large uncertainty range of 0.7-4.2 EUR, around the core estimate of 1.9 EUR/kg meat. This large uncertainty comes primarily from the cost fraction (80%) paid for damage on ecosystems where a translation into monetary terms is associated with an uncertainty range of 350-3500 EUR around the core value of 1400 EUR/BAHY (see Section 2.1.4). In the same manner, it appears that the environmental cost estimate in relation to 'feed production' and to 'direct energy use' is expected to have a large uncertainty while that related to 'manure utilization for crop fertilization' is relatively stable, considering the contribution of impacts on ecosystems and impacts on human to the costs related to each sub-process. production and the remaining is distributed among the other sub- processes, 'emissions from housing and manure storage', 'manure utilization for crop fertilization', 'feed transport' and 'direct energy use' by 16.3%, 3.2%, 3.7% and 1.2% respectively. The finding confirms that feed use and manure management are two key priorities for improvement. Making a breakdown of the costs related to each sub-process into three damage categories, we further find that impacts on ecosystems obtain relatively high importance for 'emissions from housing and manure storage', 'direct energy use' and especially 'feed production' with 57, 84 and 90% contribution respectively whereas impacts on human well-being tend to domi- nate the costs related to 'manure utilization for crop fertilization' with 76% contribution. Interestingly, the fact that feed production is the largest contributor to the environmental costs of pig meat production also holds for the private costs where the feed costs normally constitute more than 50% of the overall costs (Rasmussen, 2004).
In weighing impact categories against each other, 'Nature occupation' represents the main contributor (as mentioned earlier) with a share of 54.7% of the aggregated costs, followed by 'Global
warming', 'Respiratory inorganics', "Eutrophication, terrestrial' and others with a share of 21.2, 18.3, 3.1 and 2.7%, respectively.
3.2. Uncertainty of environmental cost estimates using the Stepwise2006 method

Table 3 shows that the monetarization of environmental exter- nalities of pig meat results in a large uncertainty range of 0.7-4.2 EUR, around the core estimate of 1.9 EUR/kg meat. This large uncertainty comes primarily from the cost fraction (80%) paid for damage on ecosystems where a translation into monetary terms is associated with an uncertainty range of 350-3500 EUR around the core value of 1400 EUR/BAHY (see Section 2.1.4). In the same manner, it appears that the environmental cost estimate in relation to 'feed production' and to 'direct energy use' is expected to have a large uncertainty while that related to 'manure utilization for crop fertilization' is relatively stable, considering the contribution of impacts on ecosystems and impacts on human to the costs related to each sub-process. 3.3. Environmental costs of pig production based on factors in the Stepwise2006 method versus factors used in previous studies

For a verification of the monetarization scheme used in the Stepwise2006 method versus those used in previous studies, Table 4 is constructed. In this table, we analyze life cycle emissions of 7 emission species in weight units per kg meat and convert them to environmental costs in monetary units using factors in Step- wise2006 versus those used in previous studies. The emissions we select for the comparison are the ones whose reference external cost values are available (Weidema, 2009).

<list>From Table 4, we can see that the sum of environmental costs related to 7 selected emissions based on Stepwise monetary values is 0.53 EUR/kg meat. The costs are within the reference range, 0.04-0.93 EUR/kg meat, despite the fact that different valuation approaches have been used to derive monetary conversion factors. Such a good agreement shows the reasonable applicability of the novel approach whereas an inclusion of environmental costs in relation to impacts of other emissions (e.g. copper, ammonia, nitrate, etc) and especially impact of land use proves its completeness over those used in previous studies.

30: <header>3.1. Environmental costs of pig meat in the baseline scenario </header>

Starting with the baseline scenario, Table 3 shows the midpoint and endpoint results for 16 impact categories and the external costs or environmental costs which are a sum of all impact scores. The endpoint results in relation to the three safeguard subjects, ecosystems, human well-being, and resource productivity, are also evaluated and aggregated into a single score (for each impact category and for all categories combined). In addition, Fig. 2 gives a pictorial observation of main contributing sub-processes among the listed sub-processes (e.g. feed production and transport, direct energy use, emissions from housing and manure storage, manure utilization for crop fertilization) and main contributing midpoint impact categories. Detailed discussions on the findings concerning the environmental costs of conventional pig meat as well as the main contributing sub-processes and main contributing impact categories are given below.

As a whole, the environmental costs of conventional pig meat are estimated at 1.9 EUR per kg, which are of even larger size than the private costs of 1.4 EUR. Interestingly, with a brief look, we observe that more than half of the costs are responsible by 'Nature occupation' (commonly referred to as 'Land use') associated with all process activities. This stresses the importance of land use impacts associated with agricultural production which have not been properly assessed in literature due mainly to methodological problems. A breakdown of the external costs into three damage categories shows that impacts on ecosystems obtain high impor- tance with 80% contribution to the costs whereas impacts on human well-being and impacts on resource productivity carry relatively low importance with 15% and 5% contribution, respectively.

As for the detailed information about on which part (or component) of the pig meat life cycle, the highest load of different environmental impacts occurs or the largest potential for improvements lies, further analysis is needed. Fig. 2 summarizes the results of such analysis. Clearly from the figure, feed production is an important contributor to 'Nature occupation' (100%), 'Eutro- phication, aquatic' (82.5%), 'Non-renewable energy' (69%), and 'Global warming' (55%). Emissions from housing and manure storage (primarily CH4, N20 and NH3) contribute a relatively large portion to 'Photochemical ozone, vegetation' (54.6%), 'Eutrophica- tion, terrestrial' (46.2%), and 'Acidification' (44%). In contrast, manure utilization for crop fertilization has a negative contribution to (i.e. a deduction from) the environmental costs of the pig meat product in three impact categories: Non-renewable energy (10%), Global warming (5.3%) and Photochemical ozone, vegetation (5.8%) but a positive contribution (i.e. an addition) in others, notably Ecotoxicity, aquatic (92%) and Human toxicity, non-carcinogens (44%). Feed transport is not a major contributor to all impact categories except for 'Ecotoxicity, terrestrial' where its contribution reaches 81%. Direct energy use with a 14% contribution to 'Non- renewable energy' and a less-than-5% contribution to other impact categories can also be considered a minor contributor to the overall environmental burden of the product.

31: <header>3.1. Environmental costs of pig meat in the baseline scenario </header>

Starting with the baseline scenario, Table 3 shows the midpoint and endpoint results for 16 impact categories and the external costs or environmental costs which are a sum of all impact scores. The endpoint results in relation to the three safeguard subjects, ecosystems, human well-being, and resource productivity, are also evaluated and aggregated into a single score (for each impact category and for all categories combined). In addition, Fig. 2 gives a pictorial observation of main contributing sub-processes among the listed sub-processes (e.g. feed production and transport, direct energy use, emissions from housing and manure storage, manure utilization for crop fertilization) and main contributing midpoint impact categories. Detailed discussions on the findings concerning the environmental costs of conventional pig meat as well as the main contributing sub-processes and main contributing impact categories are given below.

As a whole, the environmental costs of conventional pig meat are estimated at 1.9 EUR per kg, which are of even larger size than the private costs of 1.4 EUR. Interestingly, with a brief look, we observe that more than half of the costs are responsible by 'Nature occupation' (commonly referred to as 'Land use') associated with all process activities. This stresses the importance of land use impacts associated with agricultural production which have not been properly assessed in literature due mainly to methodological problems. A breakdown of the external costs into three damage categories shows that impacts on ecosystems obtain high impor- tance with 80% contribution to the costs whereas impacts on human well-being and impacts on resource productivity carry relatively low importance with 15% and 5% contribution, respectively.

As for the detailed information about on which part (or component) of the pig meat life cycle, the highest load of different environmental impacts occurs or the largest potential for improvements lies, further analysis is needed. Fig. 2 summarizes the results of such analysis. Clearly from the figure, feed production is an important contributor to 'Nature occupation' (100%), 'Eutro- phication, aquatic' (82.5%), 'Non-renewable energy' (69%), and 'Global warming' (55%). Emissions from housing and manure storage (primarily CH4, N20 and NH3) contribute a relatively large portion to 'Photochemical ozone, vegetation' (54.6%), 'Eutrophica- tion, terrestrial' (46.2%), and 'Acidification' (44%). In contrast, manure utilization for crop fertilization has a negative contribution to (i.e. a deduction from) the environmental costs of the pig meat product in three impact categories: Non-renewable energy (10%), Global warming (5.3%) and Photochemical ozone, vegetation (5.8%) but a positive contribution (i.e. an addition) in others, notably Ecotoxicity, aquatic (92%) and Human toxicity, non-carcinogens (44%). Feed transport is not a major contributor to all impact categories except for 'Ecotoxicity, terrestrial' where its contribution reaches 81%. Direct energy use with a 14% contribution to 'Non- renewable energy' and a less-than-5% contribution to other impact categories can also be considered a minor contributor to the overall environmental burden of the product.

production and the remaining is distributed among the other sub- processes, 'emissions from housing and manure storage', 'manure utilization for crop fertilization', 'feed transport' and 'direct energy use' by 16.3%, 3.2%, 3.7% and 1.2% respectively. The finding confirms that feed use and manure management are two key priorities for improvement. Making a breakdown of the costs related to each sub-process into three damage categories, we further find that impacts on ecosystems obtain relatively high importance for 'emissions from housing and manure storage', 'direct energy use' and especially 'feed production' with 57, 84 and 90% contribution respectively whereas impacts on human well-being tend to domi- nate the costs related to 'manure utilization for crop fertilization' with 76% contribution. Interestingly, the fact that feed production is the largest contributor to the environmental costs of pig meat production also holds for the private costs where the feed costs normally constitute more than 50% of the overall costs (Rasmussen, 2004).
In weighing impact categories against each other, 'Nature occupation' represents the main contributor (as mentioned earlier) with a share of 54.7% of the aggregated costs, followed by 'Global
warming', 'Respiratory inorganics', "Eutrophication, terrestrial' and others with a share of 21.2, 18.3, 3.1 and 2.7%, respectively.
3.2. Uncertainty of environmental cost estimates using the Stepwise2006 method

Table 3 shows that the monetarization of environmental exter- nalities of pig meat results in a large uncertainty range of 0.7-4.2 EUR, around the core estimate of 1.9 EUR/kg meat. This large uncertainty comes primarily from the cost fraction (80%) paid for damage on ecosystems where a translation into monetary terms is associated with an uncertainty range of 350-3500 EUR around the core value of 1400 EUR/BAHY (see Section 2.1.4). In the same manner, it appears that the environmental cost estimate in relation to 'feed production' and to 'direct energy use' is expected to have a large uncertainty while that related to 'manure utilization for crop fertilization' is relatively stable, considering the contribution of impacts on ecosystems and impacts on human to the costs related to each sub-process. 3.3. Environmental costs of pig production based on factors in the Stepwise2006 method versus factors used in previous studies

For a verification of the monetarization scheme used in the Stepwise2006 method versus those used in previous studies, Table 4 is constructed. In this table, we analyze life cycle emissions of 7 emission species in weight units per kg meat and convert them to environmental costs in monetary units using factors in Step- wise2006 versus those used in previous studies. The emissions we select for the comparison are the ones whose reference external cost values are available (Weidema, 2009).

<list>From Table 4, we can see that the sum of environmental costs related to 7 selected emissions based on Stepwise monetary values is 0.53 EUR/kg meat. The costs are within the reference range, 0.04-0.93 EUR/kg meat, despite the fact that different valuation approaches have been used to derive monetary conversion factors. Such a good agreement shows the reasonable applicability of the novel approach whereas an inclusion of environmental costs in relation to impacts of other emissions (e.g. copper, ammonia, nitrate, etc) and especially impact of land use proves its completeness over those used in previous studies.

production and the remaining is distributed among the other sub- processes, 'emissions from housing and manure storage', 'manure utilization for crop fertilization', 'feed transport' and 'direct energy use' by 16.3%, 3.2%, 3.7% and 1.2% respectively. The finding confirms that feed use and manure management are two key priorities for improvement. Making a breakdown of the costs related to each sub-process into three damage categories, we further find that impacts on ecosystems obtain relatively high importance for 'emissions from housing and manure storage', 'direct energy use' and especially 'feed production' with 57, 84 and 90% contribution respectively whereas impacts on human well-being tend to domi- nate the costs related to 'manure utilization for crop fertilization' with 76% contribution. Interestingly, the fact that feed production is the largest contributor to the environmental costs of pig meat production also holds for the private costs where the feed costs normally constitute more than 50% of the overall costs (Rasmussen, 2004).
In weighing impact categories against each other, 'Nature occupation' represents the main contributor (as mentioned earlier) with a share of 54.7% of the aggregated costs, followed by 'Global
warming', 'Respiratory inorganics', "Eutrophication, terrestrial' and others with a share of 21.2, 18.3, 3.1 and 2.7%, respectively.
3.2. Uncertainty of environmental cost estimates using the Stepwise2006 method

Table 3 shows that the monetarization of environmental exter- nalities of pig meat results in a large uncertainty range of 0.7-4.2 EUR, around the core estimate of 1.9 EUR/kg meat. This large uncertainty comes primarily from the cost fraction (80%) paid for damage on ecosystems where a translation into monetary terms is associated with an uncertainty range of 350-3500 EUR around the core value of 1400 EUR/BAHY (see Section 2.1.4). In the same manner, it appears that the environmental cost estimate in relation to 'feed production' and to 'direct energy use' is expected to have a large uncertainty while that related to 'manure utilization for crop fertilization' is relatively stable, considering the contribution of impacts on ecosystems and impacts on human to the costs related to each sub-process. 3.3. Environmental costs of pig production based on factors in the Stepwise2006 method versus factors used in previous studies

For a verification of the monetarization scheme used in the Stepwise2006 method versus those used in previous studies, Table 4 is constructed. In this table, we analyze life cycle emissions of 7 emission species in weight units per kg meat and convert them to environmental costs in monetary units using factors in Step- wise2006 versus those used in previous studies. The emissions we select for the comparison are the ones whose reference external cost values are available (Weidema, 2009).

<list>From Table 4, we can see that the sum of environmental costs related to 7 selected emissions based on Stepwise monetary values is 0.53 EUR/kg meat. The costs are within the reference range, 0.04-0.93 EUR/kg meat, despite the fact that different valuation approaches have been used to derive monetary conversion factors. Such a good agreement shows the reasonable applicability of the novel approach whereas an inclusion of environmental costs in relation to impacts of other emissions (e.g. copper, ammonia, nitrate, etc) and especially impact of land use proves its completeness over those used in previous studies.

33: <header>3.1. Environmental costs of pig meat in the baseline scenario </header>

Starting with the baseline scenario, Table 3 shows the midpoint and endpoint results for 16 impact categories and the external costs or environmental costs which are a sum of all impact scores. The endpoint results in relation to the three safeguard subjects, ecosystems, human well-being, and resource productivity, are also evaluated and aggregated into a single score (for each impact category and for all categories combined). In addition, Fig. 2 gives a pictorial observation of main contributing sub-processes among the listed sub-processes (e.g. feed production and transport, direct energy use, emissions from housing and manure storage, manure utilization for crop fertilization) and main contributing midpoint impact categories. Detailed discussions on the findings concerning the environmental costs of conventional pig meat as well as the main contributing sub-processes and main contributing impact categories are given below.

As a whole, the environmental costs of conventional pig meat are estimated at 1.9 EUR per kg, which are of even larger size than the private costs of 1.4 EUR. Interestingly, with a brief look, we observe that more than half of the costs are responsible by 'Nature occupation' (commonly referred to as 'Land use') associated with all process activities. This stresses the importance of land use impacts associated with agricultural production which have not been properly assessed in literature due mainly to methodological problems. A breakdown of the external costs into three damage categories shows that impacts on ecosystems obtain high impor- tance with 80% contribution to the costs whereas impacts on human well-being and impacts on resource productivity carry relatively low importance with 15% and 5% contribution, respectively.

As for the detailed information about on which part (or component) of the pig meat life cycle, the highest load of different environmental impacts occurs or the largest potential for improvements lies, further analysis is needed. Fig. 2 summarizes the results of such analysis. Clearly from the figure, feed production is an important contributor to 'Nature occupation' (100%), 'Eutro- phication, aquatic' (82.5%), 'Non-renewable energy' (69%), and 'Global warming' (55%). Emissions from housing and manure storage (primarily CH4, N20 and NH3) contribute a relatively large portion to 'Photochemical ozone, vegetation' (54.6%), 'Eutrophica- tion, terrestrial' (46.2%), and 'Acidification' (44%). In contrast, manure utilization for crop fertilization has a negative contribution to (i.e. a deduction from) the environmental costs of the pig meat product in three impact categories: Non-renewable energy (10%), Global warming (5.3%) and Photochemical ozone, vegetation (5.8%) but a positive contribution (i.e. an addition) in others, notably Ecotoxicity, aquatic (92%) and Human toxicity, non-carcinogens (44%). Feed transport is not a major contributor to all impact categories except for 'Ecotoxicity, terrestrial' where its contribution reaches 81%. Direct energy use with a 14% contribution to 'Non- renewable energy' and a less-than-5% contribution to other impact categories can also be considered a minor contributor to the overall environmental burden of the product.

This stresses the importance of land use impacts associated with agricultural production which have not been properly assessed in literature due mainly to methodological problems. A breakdown of the external costs into three damage categories shows that impacts on ecosystems obtain high impor- tance with 80% contribution to the costs whereas impacts on human well-being and impacts on resource productivity carry relatively low importance with 15% and 5% contribution, respectively.

As for the detailed information about on which part (or component) of the pig meat life cycle, the highest load of different environmental impacts occurs or the largest potential for improvements lies, further analysis is needed. Fig. 2 summarizes the results of such analysis. Clearly from the figure, feed production is an important contributor to 'Nature occupation' (100%), 'Eutro- phication, aquatic' (82.5%), 'Non-renewable energy' (69%), and 'Global warming' (55%). Emissions from housing and manure storage (primarily CH4, N20 and NH3) contribute a relatively large portion to 'Photochemical ozone, vegetation' (54.6%), 'Eutrophica- tion, terrestrial' (46.2%), and 'Acidification' (44%). In contrast, manure utilization for crop fertilization has a negative contribution to (i.e. a deduction from) the environmental costs of the pig meat product in three impact categories: Non-renewable energy (10%), Global warming (5.3%) and Photochemical ozone, vegetation (5.8%) but a positive contribution (i.e. an addition) in others, notably Ecotoxicity, aquatic (92%) and Human toxicity, non-carcinogens (44%). Feed transport is not a major contributor to all impact categories except for 'Ecotoxicity, terrestrial' where its contribution reaches 81%. Direct energy use with a 14% contribution to 'Non- renewable energy' and a less-than-5% contribution to other impact categories can also be considered a minor contributor to the overall environmental burden of the product.

production and the remaining is distributed among the other sub- processes, 'emissions from housing and manure storage', 'manure utilization for crop fertilization', 'feed transport' and 'direct energy use' by 16.3%, 3.2%, 3.7% and 1.2% respectively. The finding confirms that feed use and manure management are two key priorities for improvement. Making a breakdown of the costs related to each sub-process into three damage categories, we further find that impacts on ecosystems obtain relatively high importance for 'emissions from housing and manure storage', 'direct energy use' and especially 'feed production' with 57, 84 and 90% contribution respectively whereas impacts on human well-being tend to domi- nate the costs related to 'manure utilization for crop fertilization' with 76% contribution. Interestingly, the fact that feed production is the largest contributor to the environmental costs of pig meat production also holds for the private costs where the feed costs normally constitute more than 50% of the overall costs (Rasmussen, 2004).
In weighing impact categories against each other, 'Nature occupation' represents the main contributor (as mentioned earlier) with a share of 54.7% of the aggregated costs, followed by 'Global
warming', 'Respiratory inorganics', "Eutrophication, terrestrial' and others with a share of 21.2, 18.3, 3.1 and 2.7%, respectively.
3.2. Uncertainty of environmental cost estimates using the Stepwise2006 method

Table 3 shows that the monetarization of environmental exter- nalities of pig meat results in a large uncertainty range of 0.7-4.2 EUR, around the core estimate of 1.9 EUR/kg meat. This large uncertainty comes primarily from the cost fraction (80%) paid for damage on ecosystems where a translation into monetary terms is associated with an uncertainty range of 350-3500 EUR around the core value of 1400 EUR/BAHY (see Section 2.1.4). In the same manner, it appears that the environmental cost estimate in relation to 'feed production' and to 'direct energy use' is expected to have a large uncertainty while that related to 'manure utilization for crop fertilization' is relatively stable, considering the contribution of impacts on ecosystems and impacts on human to the costs related to each sub-process. 3.3. Environmental costs of pig production based on factors in the Stepwise2006 method versus factors used in previous studies

For a verification of the monetarization scheme used in the Stepwise2006 method versus those used in previous studies, Table 4 is constructed. In this table, we analyze life cycle emissions of 7 emission species in weight units per kg meat and convert them to environmental costs in monetary units using factors in Step- wise2006 versus those used in previous studies. The emissions we select for the comparison are the ones whose reference external cost values are available (Weidema, 2009).

<list>From Table 4, we can see that the sum of environmental costs related to 7 selected emissions based on Stepwise monetary values is 0.53 EUR/kg meat. The costs are within the reference range, 0.04-0.93 EUR/kg meat, despite the fact that different valuation approaches have been used to derive monetary conversion factors. Such a good agreement shows the reasonable applicability of the novel approach whereas an inclusion of environmental costs in relation to impacts of other emissions (e.g. copper, ammonia, nitrate, etc) and especially impact of land use proves its completeness over those used in previous studies.

Starting with the baseline scenario, Table 3 shows the midpoint and endpoint results for 16 impact categories and the external costs or environmental costs which are a sum of all impact scores. The endpoint results in relation to the three safeguard subjects, ecosystems, human well-being, and resource productivity, are also evaluated and aggregated into a single score (for each impact category and for all categories combined). In addition, Fig. 2 gives a pictorial observation of main contributing sub-processes among the listed sub-processes (e.g. feed production and transport, direct energy use, emissions from housing and manure storage, manure utilization for crop fertilization) and main contributing midpoint impact categories. Detailed discussions on the findings concerning the environmental costs of conventional pig meat as well as the main contributing sub-processes and main contributing impact categories are given below.

As a whole, the environmental costs of conventional pig meat are estimated at 1.9 EUR per kg, which are of even larger size than the private costs of 1.4 EUR. Interestingly, with a brief look, we observe that more than half of the costs are responsible by 'Nature occupation' (commonly referred to as 'Land use') associated with all process activities. This stresses the importance of land use impacts associated with agricultural production which have not been properly assessed in literature due mainly to methodological problems. A breakdown of the external costs into three damage categories shows that impacts on ecosystems obtain high impor- tance with 80% contribution to the costs whereas impacts on human well-being and impacts on resource productivity carry relatively low importance with 15% and 5% contribution, respectively.

As for the detailed information about on which part (or component) of the pig meat life cycle, the highest load of different environmental impacts occurs or the largest potential for improvements lies, further analysis is needed. Fig. 2 summarizes the results of such analysis. Clearly from the figure, feed production is an important contributor to 'Nature occupation' (100%), 'Eutro- phication, aquatic' (82.5%), 'Non-renewable energy' (69%), and 'Global warming' (55%). Emissions from housing and manure storage (primarily CH4, N20 and NH3) contribute a relatively large portion to 'Photochemical ozone, vegetation' (54.6%), 'Eutrophica- tion, terrestrial' (46.2%), and 'Acidification' (44%). In contrast, manure utilization for crop fertilization has a negative contribution to (i.e. a deduction from) the environmental costs of the pig meat product in three impact categories: Non-renewable energy (10%), Global warming (5.3%) and Photochemical ozone, vegetation (5.8%) but a positive contribution (i.e. an addition) in others, notably Ecotoxicity, aquatic (92%) and Human toxicity, non-carcinogens (44%). Feed transport is not a major contributor to all impact categories except for 'Ecotoxicity, terrestrial' where its contribution reaches 81%. Direct energy use with a 14% contribution to 'Non- renewable energy' and a less-than-5% contribution to other impact categories can also be considered a minor contributor to the overall environmental burden of the product.

3.3. Environmental costs of pig production based on factors in the Stepwise2006 method versus factors used in previous studies

For a verification of the monetarization scheme used in the Stepwise2006 method versus those used in previous studies, Table 4 is constructed. In this table, we analyze life cycle emissions of 7 emission species in weight units per kg meat and convert them to environmental costs in monetary units using factors in Step- wise2006 versus those used in previous studies. The emissions we select for the comparison are the ones whose reference external cost values are available (Weidema, 2009).

<list>From Table 4, we can see that the sum of environmental costs related to 7 selected emissions based on Stepwise monetary values is 0.53 EUR/kg meat. The costs are within the reference range, 0.04-0.93 EUR/kg meat, despite the fact that different valuation approaches have been used to derive monetary conversion factors. Such a good agreement shows the reasonable applicability of the novel approach whereas an inclusion of environmental costs in relation to impacts of other emissions (e.g. copper, ammonia, nitrate, etc) and especially impact of land use proves its completeness over those used in previous studies.
